query: is Mycobacterium abscessus a known human pathogen

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other identification methods include growth rates, colony variation, biochemical differences, and chromatography. In particular, NTM species that grow in liquid media within 7 days are classed as rapid growers, and those that take more than 2 weeks to grow are slow growers. [17] However, older phenotypic classifications have been replaced with more advanced molecular and genomic methods for microorganism identification due to more rapid and precise results with these newer modalities. [18] As a result, further species of NTM can be further discovered and differentiated from existing species due to genomic differences. The most commonly identified pulmonary NTM species in countries such as the US, Japan, and French Guiana include Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC), M gordonae , Mycobacteriu m kansasii , Mycobacterium fortuitum, and Mycobacterium abscessus . [19] [20] [21] See Image. Mycobacterium Kansasii.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Mycobacterial Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical mycobacteria or nontuberculous mycobacteria cause various diseases such as skin and soft tissue infection, lymphadenitis, pulmonary infection, disseminated infection, and a wide range of more rarely encountered infections. The most commonly encountered atypical mycobacteria that cause the majority of infections in humans are the Mycobacterium avium complex bacteria (MAC), Mycobacterium avium, and Mycobacterium intracellulare, also referred to as Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare (MAI), Mycobacterium kansasii, Mycobacterium marinum, Mycobacterium ulcerans, Mycobacterium abscessus complex bacteria (abscessus, massiliense, and bolletii), Mycobacterium chelonae, and Mycobacterium fortuitum . These organisms are most commonly found in the soil and indoor and outdoor water sources and are recognized to colonize poorly sanitized medical equipment. Atypical mycobacteria most commonly infect young children, immunocompromised individuals, individuals with indwelling medical equipment, and those recently undergoing surgical or non-surgical procedures. These bacteria can be further categorized into slow and rapidly-growing organisms and grouped by the organ systems they commonly affect. Due to their morphology and growth patterns, atypical mycobacteria are challenging to identify on diagnostic testing. Treatment often requires extended courses of combination antibiotic therapy, frequently with surgical intervention. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Mycobacterial Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: One defining feature of most atypical mycobacteria is that they are acid-fast bacilli. This staining property detected via the Kinyoun method is due to its hydrophobic mycolic acid outer layer. This mycolic acid layer allows them to form thick biofilms, enabling their survival in harsh environments that are not as well tolerated by other bacteria. These biofilms stunt the ability of antibiotics to penetrate bacteria effectively. They allow for survival in varying temperatures and protect the bacteria from being killed by water-purifying chemicals such as chlorine, disinfectants, and cleaning products. Biofilms also allow the bacteria to survive in oxygen-rich and anaerobic environments. Another defining characteristic of mycobacteria is their slow rate of growth. Atypical mycobacteria are divided into rapid and slow-growing categories. Rapid-growing organisms take 7 to 30 days to grow on culture. Slow-growing organisms take several weeks to months to demonstrate growth. The type of disease caused by these organisms depends on the mode of inoculation and the host's susceptibility to infection. Pulmonary infection occurs via inhalation of aerosolized material containing the bacteria. In contrast, lymphadenitis is often caused by ingestion or penetration through the soft tissues, and skin and soft tissue infection by access through skin breakdown or by indwelling medical equipment. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Mycobacterial Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Pulmonary
retrieved_docs.content: Mycobacterium intracellulare has demonstrated rising minimum inhibitory concentrations and increased resistance to anti-mycobacterial agents compared to Mycobacterium avium , making it more complicated to treat. Alternative agents to treat MAC/MAI pulmonary disease include inhaled amikacin, clofazimine (as a substitute for rifampin), and moxifloxacin. Several other atypical mycobacterial species have been shown to cause pulmonary disease and are generally susceptible to macrolides, rifampin/rifabutin, streptomycin, amikacin, doxycycline, and fluoroquinolones. Notably, pulmonary disease caused by Mycobacterium abscessus is resistant to most antibiotics and can only be cured in a minority of patients by surgical resection. Those who are not cured are administered chronic intermittent antibiotic therapy to attempt to slow disease progression. [1] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Mycobacterium chelonae Infection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: M. chelonae and M. abscessus were considered identical until 1992 when M. chelonae was elevated to species status. Both M. chelonae and M. abscessus have an identical sequence in the 54-510 region but can be differentiated by their intergenic sequence (ITS), hsp65 , or gene sequences. Susceptibility profiles can be helpful, but they are not ideal in differentiating between the M. chelonae group and M. abscessus group. M. chelonae group is characterized by high MICs of cefoxitin (> 64 mg/L) and susceptibility to tobramycin (MIC ≤ 4 mg/L), whereas M. abscessus shows lower MICs of cefoxitin (≤ 64 mg/L) and resistance to tobramycin (MIC of > 8 mg/L) (3).

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Mycobacterial Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary: The 3 species that comprise the vast majority of atypical mycobacterial pulmonary infections are the Mycobacterium avium complex bacteria (approximately 80%), Mycobacterium kansasii, and Mycobacterium abscessus . Mycobacterium avium complex infections can be further grouped into 2 different presentations. The first presentation is found more commonly in middle-aged males with the risk factors of long-term alcohol and tobacco abuse. In these patients, the MAC bacteria tend to form large fibrocavitary lesions in the apex of the lung. These lesions can rapidly progress to encompass significant areas of the lung within 1 to 2 years and generally result in morbidity and mortality at a much faster rate. The second type of presentation of MAC lung disease more commonly affects postmenopausal caucasian women. In this presentation, the bacteria form small pulmonary nodules and cylindrical bronchiectasis with a concentration of nodules in the right middle lobe and ligula of the left upper lobe of the lungs. Similar presentations to MAC pulmonary disease are seen with Mycobacterium kansasii and abscessus. Mycobacterium kansasii tends to form large fibrocavities in the apex of the lung, similar to the first type of MAC disease presentation. A much smaller proportion of infections resemble the nodular and bronchiectatic type of presentation. Mycobacterium abscessus infection usually presents with a nodular and bronchiectatic presentation concentrated in the upper lung fields. Mycobacterium abscessus forms fibrocavities in approximately 15 percent of infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycobacterium avium Complex -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Mycobacterium avium complex consists of multiple nontuberculosis mycobacterial species (NTM), which cannot be distinguished in the microbiology laboratory and require genetic testing. A newcomer to the Mycobacterium avium complex is the Mycobacterium paraintracellulare, identified in pulmonary infections in Southeast Asia in 2016. M. avium complex is the most common cause of nontuberculosis mycobacterial species infections in humans, and the respiratory system is the most common site of infection. This activity reviews the presentation and pathophysiology of Mycobacterium avium intracellulare and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Mycobacterial Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When encountered by the immune system, atypical mycobacteria are taken up by macrophages, causing the macrophages to release IL-12 and TNF-alpha. IL-12 release activates the IL-12-interferon gamma pathway. This pathway further recruits additional macrophages, neutrophils, and T-cells. This process ultimately results in the killing of these intracellular bacteria by nitric oxide and free radicals. The macrophages, neutrophils, and T-cells recruited to the site of infection can sometimes result in granuloma formation, creating a fibrinous mass walling off the infection and isolating it from the rest of the body. Atypical mycobacteria can often evade this response due to the ability of the glycoproteins in their cell wall to inactivate the phagosome-lysosome fusion process and free radical destruction, allowing the bacteria to live as intracellular organisms within macrophages.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycobacterium avium Complex -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the pathophysiology of Mycobacterium avium intracellulare. Identify the presentation of a patient with Mycobacterium avium intracellulare. Evaluate the treatment of Mycobacterium avium intracellulare. Outline modalities to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by Mycobacterium avium intracellulare. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycobacterium avium Complex -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Mycobacterial infections, including Mycobacterium avium complex infections, can be categorized into several clinical patterns, including pulmonary disease, skin and soft tissue infections, musculoskeletal infections, disseminated disease, catheter-associated disease, and lymphadenitis. Pulmonary disease is the most common presentation. Mycobacterium avium complex infections occur in both immunocompetent and immunosuppressed patients. M. avium is the most frequent organism in HIV and immunosuppressed patients; about 40% of pulmonary infections in immunocompetent patients can be due to M. intracellulare . With the addition of new members to the Mycobacterium avium complex, the extent of involvement of each organism within the Mycobacterium avium complex is unknown.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycobacterium chelonae Infection -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Infection with M. chelonae may be asymptomatic. Colonization of the pulmonary tract in cystic fibrosis patients is likely. M. abscessus frequently cause pulmonary infections. Skin biopsy and cultures should help to evaluate skin and subcutaneous infections on the extremities, those not responding to antibiotics, or in patients who had injection procedures or are immunocompromised. The biochemical evaluation in the microbiologic lab is inadequate to identify NTM to the species level, which is important for instituting appropriate therapy. Molecular techniques such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP) should be utilized to identify NTM including M chelonae. M. chelonae can have unpredictable resistance pattern. However, most are sensitive to macrolide and aminoglycosides. Susceptibility testing is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: While the prevalence and incidence of NTM pulmonary disease have been increasing through the years, not only in the US but also around the world, this epidemiological variation has not been fully explained. Increased awareness among treating clinicians has been proposed as a reason for increased incidence, along with an increasingly aging and comorbid population among high-income countries. [40] Environmental associations have also been postulated, with increased population exposure to plumbing, indoor swimming pools, high rainfall events, and disruption of local soil being linked with a higher risk of NTM infections. [37] [41] [42] [43] [44] Hot tubs themselves have been clinically linked with cases of MAC. [45] New radiological advances, especially resolution chest computed tomography (CT) scanning, improved diagnosis, and increased chest screening may also be important factors responsible for these epidemiological observations. [46] [47] See Image. Chest CT of Mycobacterium Abscessus.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In those without predisposing structural lung disease, low body mass index, scoliosis, and thoracic cage abnormalities have been associated with NTM pulmonary disease. [57] [58] [59] Gastroesophageal reflux disease has been associated with pulmonary NTM infection, possibly as a result of the survivability of mycobacteria in low-pH environments. [60] Unlike tuberculosis, NTM pulmonary disease is not thought to be transmitted person-to-person or by exposure to droplets from an infected individual. [61] That all pulmonary NTM become acquired via inhalation of infected aerosolized droplets from the environment or water sources has been accepted. Household water and shower aerosols are known to be colonized with various NTM species, with the most frequently cited being M avium, M kansasii, M abscessus , and Mycobacterium lentiflavum . [62] Similarly, NTM has been isolated throughout the year in natural and municipal potable water in major cities globally, with species differing according to season. [63] Once the organisms enter the individual, they usually settle in the lower airways; in some cases, the bacteria incite an inflammatory reaction with an influx of lymphocytes. The resulting release of cytokines and other mediators can lead to an infectious process that presents as pneumonia. With an insufficient immune response to contain these mycobacteria, the pathogen can disseminate throughout the human host and cause extrapulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a fragile immune system are more prone to worse outcomes when compared with immunocompetent patients. Furthermore, pulmonary NTM patients with comorbidities have been observed to have survival expectancy following diagnosis reduced to as low as 8.6 years compared to those without comorbidities. [125] The method of diagnosis has also been shown to correlate with disease progression, with patients who have NTM pulmonary disease diagnosed through means other than sputum having a lower risk of disease progression over 5 years compared to those diagnosed by sputum culture. [126] The prognosis also depends on the type of NTM lung infection. Studies have shown that patients with MAC lung infections have a better prognosis when compared to patients with other NTM infections. [127] Conversely, the mortality rate of patients with pulmonary infections with M abscessus is greater when compared with patients with other NTM lung infections. [128] Other prognostic risk factors for mortality identified on multivariable analyses from 15-year cohort studies include older age ≥65 years, male sex, a body mass index <18.5 kg per square meter, underlying chronic cardiac and liver diseases, chronic pulmonary aspergillosis and cavitary lung disease at presentation. [129]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Abscess -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with alcohol use disorder, the most common underlying organisms of a lung abscess include Staphylococcus aureus , Klebsiella pneumoniae , Streptococcus pyogenes , and Actinomyces . Poor dental hygiene is an independent risk factor for the development of lung abscesses. [6] The incidence of Mycobacterium abscessus complex infection is greater in lung transplant recipients than in other solid organ transplant recipients. This increased incidence is primarily because lung transplant recipients need higher levels of immunosuppression posttransplant than other solid organ transplant recipients. Additionally, lung transplant recipients possess numerous donor-derived dendritic cells capable of activating T-cells and may encounter environmental antigens that could lead to graft rejection. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Eales Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: One study hypothesized that Mycobacterium chelonae and M. fortuitum , which are rapidly growing nontuberculous mycobacteria (RGNTM), are associated with Eales disease. [10] M. fortuitum has been isolated from the aqueous humor of a patient with Eales disease. [10] This study was undertaken to determine the presence of the DNA of M.tuberculosis and RGNTM in the vitreous humor of patients having Eales disease. [10] Semi-nested polymerase chain reactions (SnPCRs) were used to detect DNA on epiretinal membranes (ERMs) and vitreous humor of patients with Eales disease and in control patients. [10] Routine investigations for mycobacteriological profile were also done. 70% of samples of the epiretinal membrane tested positive for one or more species of Mycobacteria. [10] Vitreous humor samples also yielded M. chelonae and M. fortuitum . [10] A statistically significant association between the Eales disease and Mycobacteria was shown by this study. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is identified as a finding located in the lung, indicating the presence of an abscess that may extend into the mediastinum. Bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess is a therapeutic procedure that, while performed via the airway, has an indirect procedural site in the lung, reflecting its role in managing pulmonary abscesses. Computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate also represents a finding in the lung, revealing a diffuse pattern of tiny nodular infiltrates that suggest widespread pulmonary involvement. Together, these entities underscore the lung as the central anatomic site for both diagnostic findings (abscess and miliary infiltrates) and interventional management (bronchoscopic drainage) of serious pulmonary infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease, and other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess). Consequently, lung and mediastinal abscesses belong to the broader category of lower respiratory conditions that are linked to the formation of abscesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 972
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is categorized as an “other lower respiratory disease,” placing it within the broader group of segmental respiratory disorders (اضطراب منصفي). The mediastinum serves as the anatomical location of these other lower respiratory diseases and is the direct site examined by thoracic computed tomography scans that detect mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, whether unilateral (right or left) or bilateral. These lower respiratory conditions are closely associated with the presence of an abscess (خراج), reflecting the infectious nature of many mediastinal pathologies. Additionally, a computed tomography of the chest that demonstrates mediastinal effusion is itself classified as a segmental disorder (اضطراب منصفي), further linking imaging findings to the same disease hierarchy. Together, these relationships highlight the mediastinum’s central role in segmental lower respiratory diseases, the frequent association with abscess formation, and the importance of thoracic CT imaging for identifying complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas and effusions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 837
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease. The category other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is itself a subcategory of اضطراب منصفي. In addition, the imaging finding computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Thus, both the specific clinical entity Abces van long en mediastinum and the radiologic manifestation mediastinal effusion are encompassed within the broader disorder اضطراب منصفي, highlighting the link between lower respiratory infections, abscess formation, and mediastinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1213
retrieved_docs.content: Other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is classified as a subtype of اضطراب منصفي. The imaging finding described as computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is also identified as an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Consequently, both the clinical entity other lower respiratory disease and the radiologic observation of mediastinal effusion share the same broader disorder category, اضطراب منصفي, while the respiratory disease specifically carries a risk of developing an abscess.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum is identified as a principal anatomic site for “other lower respiratory disease,” a category that includes pulmonary and mediastinal abscesses (خراج) and segmental disorders (اضطراب منصفي). Within this spectrum, mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية) represent localized growths that may arise in the mid, posterior, or superior mediastinum, and can contain calcium (mediastinal mass containing calcium). Mediastinal effusion is also classified as اضطراب منصفي. Chest computed tomography (CT) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating these mediastinal pathologies. The parent concept “computerized axial tomography (CT) scan” encompasses chest CT, which includes sub‑procedures such as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast and diagnostic thoracic CT performed without contrast and subsequently with contrast. Chest CT (also referred to as thoracic CT scan) is routinely used to detect and characterize mediastinal‑pleural fistulas—documented as unilateral right or left fistulas, bilateral fistulas, and specific mediastinal‑pleural fistula entities—and to precisely localize mediastinal masses in the mid, posterior, or superior compartments. Related chest imaging procedures, such as chest X‑ray studies, are linked as parent procedures within the chest imaging hierarchy. Collectively, these relationships underscore the central role of chest CT in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing a range of mediastinal diseases, from infectious abscesses and segmental disorders to calcium‑bearing neoplastic masses and complex fistulous complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease, is an infectious abscess (خراج) located in the mediastinum and is hierarchically linked to both اضطراب منصفي (mediastinal disorder) and كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). The mediastinum therefore serves as the primary anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory pathologies that include mediastinal abscesses, mediastinal effusion (another form of اضطراب منصفي), and various mediastinal masses such as mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, superior‑mediastinal, generic mediastinal masses, and mediastinal masses containing calcium. All of these entities are identified and classified using computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest), a thoracic CT protocol that also detects mediastinal‑pleural fistulas and their laterality variants (right‑sided, left‑sided, or bilateral). In addition, breast CT is grouped under the broader category of chest CT because it employs the same imaging technology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that chest CT is the essential diagnostic platform for visualizing mediastinal effusions, diverse mediastinal masses (including calcified lesions), and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, thereby guiding clinical decision‑making and procedural planning for mediastinal and lower respiratory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2868
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess is an interventional procedure that is performed indirectly on the plíce (lung), allowing clinicians to access and treat infectious collections within this organ. A computed tomography of chest that reveals a miliary pulmonary infiltrate identifies abnormal radiographic findings located in the plíce, indicating a diffuse pattern of tiny nodular lesions that may be associated with infectious, inflammatory, or metastatic processes. The plíce also serves as the anatomical origin for سرطان الغدة الرئوية (pulmonary gland carcinoma), a malignant neoplasm arising from glandular tissue within the lung. Together, these concepts illustrate how the lung is both the site of diagnostic imaging findings, the target of therapeutic bronchoscopy for abscess drainage, and the primary organ from which specific lung cancers develop, underscoring the interconnected role of imaging, procedural intervention, and oncology in pulmonary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 190
retrieved_docs.content: CT in identifying a wide range of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities. Collectively, these relationships delineate an integrated framework in which mediastinal pathology—ranging from abscesses, segmental disorders, and various mediastinal masses (including calcium‑bearing lesions) to pleural‑mediastinal fistulas and miliary infiltrates—is situated within the category of other lower respiratory disease and is primarily evaluated through chest CT imaging. The hierarchical structure links general CT scanning to specialized chest CT procedures, to specific diagnostic findings, while positioning conventional chest x‑ray as a related but subordinate imaging option, thereby supporting comprehensive assessment and management of mediastinal and lower‑respiratory conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: The first three entities are categorized as mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), while mediastinal effusion is classified as a mediastinal disorder (اضطراب منصفي). Chest CT also detects complications of the mediastinum, notably mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, which may be unilateral, bilateral, right‑sided, or left‑sided. These fistulous findings underscore the role of CT in diagnosing mediastinal‑pleural communication. In addition, CT imaging of the chest can reveal lung and mediastinal abscesses (Abces van long en mediastinum), which are grouped under other lower respiratory diseases and are linked to abscess formation (خراج) and segmental disorders (اضطراب منصفي). The association between other lower respiratory diseases and mediastinal masses suggests that certain lower respiratory conditions may present as mediastinal masses. While chest X‑ray examinations are related complementary radiographic modalities, they are not direct instances of chest CT. CT of the breast, although part of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: Lung and mediastinal abscess (Abces van long en mediastinum) is described as a manifestation of other lower respiratory disease, which is linked to the segmental disorder اضطراب منصفي and characteristically presents with an abscess (خراج). The mediastinum serves as the anatomic site of this disease and the direct procedural target for thoracic computed tomography (CT) scans that evaluate mediastinal pathology, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right‑sided, left‑sided, or bilateral) and mediastinal effusion (also classified as اضطراب منصفي). Chest CT modalities such as contrast‑enhanced CT angiography of the thorax and computerized axial tomography of the thorax with contrast are the primary imaging techniques for detecting and characterizing mediastinal abnormalities, including masses located in the superior, mid, or posterior mediastinal compartments, mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية) that contain calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (CT scan chest) is categorized under “Imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract,” which also encompasses related child procedures such as pulmonary angiographic imaging (صورة وعائية رئوية). Although CT of the breast is a CT‑based technique, it is primarily associated with breast radiography rather than thoracic evaluation. Overall, the framework links the disease entities (lung‑mediastinal abscess, other lower respiratory disease, segmental disorder), anatomical location (mediastinum), imaging modalities (thoracic CT, contrast‑enhanced chest CT, pulmonary angiography), and specific radiologic findings (mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, mediastinal masses, calcium‑containing masses, mediastinal effusion) into an integrated diagnostic pathway for thoracic and mediastinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: as masses of the mid and posterior mediastinum. It is also the primary imaging technique for identifying mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, encompassing unilateral right and left presentations, bilateral involvement, and combined mediastinal‑pleural fistula findings. This integrated framework links disease entities, anatomical locations, associated complications, and imaging modalities, supporting comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic planning for mediastinal and lower respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes lung and mediastinal abscess (Abces van long en mediastinum) as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease, which is categorized under اضطراب منصفي and inversely linked to كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). Chest computed tomography (CT), including non‑contrast thoracic CT, CT of the chest (التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر), and contrast‑enhanced thoracic CT, is identified as the primary diagnostic method for a range of mediastinal abnormalities. These imaging procedures, organized under the broader Computerized Axial Tomography (CT) hierarchy and related to other chest‑x‑ray modalities, reveal findings such as mediastinal masses (mid mediastinum, posterior mediastinum, superior mediastinum, calcium‑containing mass), mediastinal effusion (classified as اضطراب منصفي), and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right‑sided, left‑sided, bilateral, or unspecified). The relationships delineate how specific respiratory infections, anatomical locations, and imaging techniques interrelate in the evaluation and management of mediastinal and lower respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease, situating an abscess that involves both lung and mediastinum within the broader spectrum of lower respiratory pathology. The mediastinum is identified as the anatomic location for this disease category. Other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and hierarchically linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder), and it is inversely related to كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass), indicating that mediastinal masses represent a specific manifestation within the lower respiratory disorder spectrum. Thoracic computed tomography, denoted as “ct scan chest,” is organized under إجراءات تصوير متعلقة بمنطقة الصدر والسبيل التنفسي (imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract) and serves as the parent modality for related studies such as صورة وعائية رئوية (pulmonary angiographic image). CT of breast, although primarily a breast imaging study, is considered a type of CT scan
